name: Associativity of multiplicative forest mappings
statement_text:
Let α, β and γ be multiplicative mappings from forests to
reals. Then
                        (αβ)γ = α(βγ).
proof_text:
Proof. If Q  R  S then (R \ Q)  (S \ Q). Hence, we find
                     
      ((αβ)γ)(S) =      (αβ)(S \ Q)γ(Q)
                       Q S
                               
                   =                     α((S \ Q) \ (R \ Q))β(R \ Q)γ(Q)
                       Q S (R\Q) (S\Q)
                       
                   =            α(S \ R)β(R \ Q)γ(Q)
                       Q RR S
                       
                   =         α(S \ R)(βγ)(R)
                       R S
                   = (α(βγ))(S).
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
If $Q \subseteq R \subseteq S$ then $(R \setminus Q) \subseteq (S \setminus Q)$. Hence, we find
\begin{align*}
((\alpha\beta)\gamma)(S) &= \sum_{Q \subseteq S} (\alpha\beta)(S \setminus Q)\gamma(Q) \\
&= \sum_{Q \subseteq S} \sum_{(R \setminus Q) \subseteq (S \setminus Q)} \alpha((S \setminus Q) \setminus (R \setminus Q))\beta(R \setminus Q)\gamma(Q) \\
&= \sum_{Q \subseteq R \subseteq S} \alpha(S \setminus R)\beta(R \setminus Q)\gamma(Q) \\
&= \sum_{R \subseteq S} \alpha(S \setminus R)(\beta\gamma)(R) \\
&= (\alpha(\beta\gamma))(S).
\end{align*}

We can now restrict multiplication to trees, and we note that associativity still remains. The semi-group that has been constructed on the set $G_{1}$ is actually a group because we can construct both left and right inverses, $\alpha_{\text{left}}^{-1}$ and $\alpha_{\text{right}}^{-1}$ say, for any $\alpha \in G_{1}$, which must be equal because
\[
\alpha_{\text{left}}^{-1} = \alpha_{\text{left}}^{-1} (\alpha \alpha_{\text{right}}^{-1}) = (\alpha_{\text{left}}^{-1} \alpha) \alpha_{\text{right}}^{-1} = \alpha_{\text{right}}^{-1}.
\]
\end{proof}

variables.name: α, β, γ
variables.definition: multiplicative mappings from forests to real numbers

variables.name: αβ
variables.definition: product of multiplicative mappings α and β, defined by (αβ)(S) = Σ_{R ≤ S} α(S \ R)β(R)

variables.name: βγ
variables.definition: product of multiplicative mappings β and γ, defined analogously to αβ

variables.name: R ≤ S
variables.definition: R is a subforest of S, meaning the vertex set and edge set of R are subsets of those of S, with the condition that if an edge is in R, both its vertices are in R

variables.name: S \ R
variables.definition: the forest induced by the difference of the vertex sets of S and R

preamble: The context involves forests (graphs that are disjoint unions of trees) and multiplicative mappings from forests to real numbers. A product of two such mappings α and β is defined via a sum over subforests R of S, using the forest difference S \ R.

dependencies.name: Existence of Left and Right Inverses
